reviews.review_description:
The five star reviews are not to be believed - unless you want to trust paid bots and clowns that have no morals. 

This game is barely mid level rogue lite at best. An arcade heavy melee game, you fight your way across new york with norse zombies invading. It's really sad that Soul Assembly again chose zombies as that's game number 27/28 - obviously this is hyperbole - that includes zombies. 

It's not fun, graphics are barely passable and overall the game just manages to exist in a time when rogue games are the highlight. If you want to spend $30+ then get a better game. If you're that hard up for a game by this dev, something containing Norse myth or thor then go nuts and waste money. But anyone giving this five stars is either paid or has no clue what quality is. 

Avoid this like their last flop last stand and save your $$

I am typically not a fan of roguelites, but the look of this game was enough to catch my interest and get me to pre-order it. And wow, it is a beautiful game for standalone. Kinda reminds me a bit of the look of The Unspoken Acolytes, for the old Rift store. And as far as it being a roguelite, there seems to at least be some progress saved. When you get to the void between realms after defeating a certain stage, I guess it saves your progress. But if you return to the void prematurely, it doesnt. I dont mind that level of roguelite. And the combat is really fun. Mjolnir has a presence to it when you're wielding it.

Overall, Id highly recommend it. A beautiful, fun, and interesting game.

reviews.review_title: Not bad. Not great, but not bad.
reviews.review_description: The games looks great and the actual story is quite interesting. The bants with Thor is good too and i rather enjoyed the combat. However... it is soooo repetitive! You fight the same enemies over and over again in the same environments. And that rune board puzzle thing between levels is just boring and completely unnecessary.  That said I've quite enjoyed the 3 hours or so that I've put into the game but I think I'll leave it there - the runs are just too repetitive.

The game softlocked before the final boss...anyhow, Street Gods is an amazing game both in its presentation and the feeling of throwing the powerful Mjolnir around. However, every other aspect of the game falters in one way or another. Resulting in a game that feels like a bigger project that was hastily squeezed down into a roguelite. From constant repetition of visiting areas, being forced to stand in place as characters talk before and after each run, and no real incentives or rewards to fight makes the game feel like a real slog. There are far better combat focused roguelites out there, so unless a new update addresses some issues I’d recommend steering clear from these streets.

Three hours in, and I nearly forgot to eat. That’s the easiest way I can sum up my first session with Street Gods on Meta Quest — a rare standalone VR purchase that actually kept me playing well past my usual one-to-two-hour refund cutoff. It’s the kind of surprise that makes you check your watch and go, “Oh — right. I’m having fun.”

The look and tone
Visually it leans hard into a graffiti-soaked cyberpunk vibe: neon paint, ragged street art, and a protagonist who could’ve been a poster for indie urban fantasy. I’ll admit I braced for the usual overbearing “girl-boss” messaging or activist baggage. Instead the game mostly lets the style speak for itself — colorful, stylized, and refreshingly light on sermonizing. That aesthetic won’t be everyone’s cup of tea (I found it slightly jarring at first), but it grows on you and gives the world character.

Weird, funny narrative bits
The game wastes no time throwing the absurd at you — you paint for five seconds and then Mjolnir appears in the street. No lore dump, no ceremony. Thor inside your hammer, Loki reduced to a googly-eyed rock — it’s dumb in a charming way and promises a story that’s willing to be patient about revealing itself.

Combat and movement
This is a melee game at heart. The hammer is the centrepiece and it’s fun to use: satisfying throws, catches, and (eventually) teleportation to the hammer. Movement is the big pivot here — early on I found traversal sluggish and a bit plodding. The moment I unlocked the teleport-to-hammer mechanic the game changed: combat tempo sped up, encounters felt punchier, and exploration became more fluid. If you’re put off by slow starting mechanics, hang in there — the mobility upgrades matter.

That said, melee impact sometimes lacks variety. Hits feel serviceable and enemies go down, but the animations and physics are a little samey: foes tend to fall in the same way and hits don’t always sell the weight you want from a mythic hammer. It’s enjoyable, but not quite the visceral, bone-shaking feedback you’d get from a highly tuned PC VR title.

Roguelike loop & pacing
Street Gods embraces roguelike repetition — lots of runs, unlocks drip in slowly, and the progression is clearly designed to be long. After three hours I discovered I was still barely out of the tutorial belt, which is great if you want longevity. Expect repetition; it’s part of the DNA, and the loop is solid enough to keep you invested.

World interaction & polish
A few little environmental interactions are delightful (hitting a car a few times and watching it explode is a small thrill), but generally the world’s interactivity is limited. Props mostly stay props — throw the hammer at a trash can and it may crumble once, then remain static. That’s a budget/standalone limitation you’ll notice sometimes: it hints at bigger physics dreams that the platform constraints can’t always satisfy.

Audio
The soundtrack is competent but unremarkable. It’s serviceable and atmospheric, but I felt it needed more punch and personality to match the visual swagger. Sound effects could use tighter, more impactful design — stronger audio feedback would go a long way toward selling those hammer hits.

How it compares
If you like current VR roguelikes (think Sweet Surrender, The Light Brigade, ZIX, Ancient Dungeon), Street Gods sits comfortably in that echelon — distinct enough to stand on its own, with a longer-form progression and a particular visual voice. It’s not the most polished melee sim, but its personality and pacing give it real charm.

Verdict
If you’re looking for a standalone Meta Quest title that’s long, quirky, and consistently fun, Street Gods is worth the buy. It scratches that roguelike melee itch, rewards persistence with meaningful movement upgrades, and has enough character to make repetitive runs feel worthwhile. With a little more polish on hit feedback, audio oomph, and world physics, it could be a standout — as it is, it’s a very good, very playable game that surprised me by keeping me glued to the headset for three straight hours.

Who should buy it: fans of VR melee and roguelike loops, solo players who enjoy slow-burn unlocks, and anyone who likes their cyberpunk a little rough-around-the-edges and a lot of personality.

Who should wait: players who demand hyper-realistic physics, ultra-varied enemy reactions, or a killer soundtrack on launch.
I love the blood in the game is the best game in the world And i Love the boss fight

Enjoying the game so far, it’s just opened up to more locations which is very welcome. Gameplay is fun in small doses. 

Still think if you like melee combat and anything Thor related, give this a go.

I was very lucky to win a game code from a competition thanks to vraimentcool.

Not sure why this only has 9 reviews, yes it’s quite repetitive, but the gameplay is very addictive, throwing the Thor Hammer feels just awesome.
I’m yet to face a boss battle yet, but loving the progression so far.
It’s totally different to any game on Quest.
It’s a yes from me,

reviews.review_title: You throw THOR’s Hammer in this game!
reviews.review_description: If that one statement doesn’t get you excited to try this game, I don’t know what else will. This game is EPIC! You smash, throw, and call back the hammer just like Thor would and call thunder strikes smite enemies! Graphics is great and the action is intense! Highly recommend!

reviews.review_title: Visually a blast, but battle repetitive
reviews.review_description: Not a bad game, but definitely felt repetitive after a bit. The whip motion only worked for me like 5-10% of the time that I attempted it, frustrating. The colors are amazing, so visually satisfying, beautiful art style. The ravens were fun. The map thing with the pieces was obnoxious because sometimes they wouldn’t grab, sometimes they were high in the air and took a while to get down, sometimes low to the floor. It was frustrating. No teleport which sucks but the movement didn’t take me out like some games. Overall not my favorite, may return and focus on multiplayer games with great fighting. I much prefer Drop Dead The Cabin from this company, absolutely stellar game (once you understand it from playing with others as the tutorial isn’tgreat) and its multiplayer.

I am having a blast with this game. The hammer handles so easily that you really feel like Thor. Once you get good, you can start intentionally missing just so you can recall the hammer and ding the enemies in the back of the head! That’s when the real fun starts! 

I play with a woojer vest, music down to mid level, and sound boosted, because the sound and music are a little bit of a let down, but get the job done with the right settings.

I actually don't mind the combat, even though it's underwhelming... Said combat is much better than the majority of vr games I've played in the past. I cannot stand the board pieces, I can't stand the runes that you need to look at from a pitch perfect angle, I think they should he just been a crystal that you collect, and I certainly cannot stand how once you are about to fight a boss, you gotta put the runes together on the board and make sure they match. All of this is just padding and I really don't think we need it. It's frustrating and it makes me dread coming back to the lobby because now it's like "ugh! I gotta do another one of these stupid puzzles."

On a side note, GET READY FOR THIS GAME TO DRAIN YOUR BATTERY! FAST!

reviews.review_title: It’s FUN ! BUT DEVS PLZ FIX BRACELET PRO
reviews.review_description: It’s a fun game once you get some ability’s unlocked like teleport . One problem I’m having is after I unlocked the shield on my left hand , I try to equipment the other bracelet, that allows me to pull enemies close to me, but I can’t change bracelets…. DEVs please fix .  I can change hammer ability just fine , but not the bracelets…

reviews.review_title: Great game only needs higher difficulty
reviews.review_description:
The game is really showing its potential once You play it longer. There are already great mechanics and production value. 

I only wish there is increased difficulty and probably more variety in levels. Also a kind of jumping would be necessary to make the battles more traversal. All that could be easily added in updates.

I hope the game would be supported by the developers post launch.
Even though you got cool abilities and stunning maps. The enemies and sometimes maps got repeated a lot of times. Maps are fine, but you should try to make different enemy models.

Very good game, Graphics are excellent, best thing is the Thor Hammer 🔨, absolutely amazing to use.
Same repeatedly maps and almost same enemies are the cons which can be fix in a update.

reviews.review_title: Love it but I have a issue
reviews.review_description: In one of the surer levels after I killed the enemies the portal didn’t spawn even though I looked everywhere
No game a high rating.  Marvel could  not have done a better job at this price point.  I am enjoying swinging and throwing my hammer.
Everything about this game is fantastic, but it is missing Quest Achievements 🏆 Hopefully updated to include soon!
nice concept, mediocre implementation.
W game buy it Now
Title says it all
